coredata.dc:description: Light emission intensity and a lifetime of perovskite light-emitting diodes (PeLEDs) have improved considerably through fine-tuning the regulation of carriers’ recombination and transport. This study found that adding poly (4-vinylpyridine) within a CsxPbBry system leads to a dipole–dipole interaction of pyridine-N and Pb2+. This interaction increases the solubility of the CsxPbBry precursor solution, which stabilizes the CsxPbBry crystal structure and induces a CsPbBr3 unitary state. The interaction further generates a polaron region and causes the orderly distribution of excess carriers in the electric field, thus preventing disordered surface charges from structurally damaging the crystal. The PEDOT:PSS hole transport layer mixed with a graphene quantum dot made of chitin tunes the highest occupied molecular orbital to improve carrier transmission efficiency. We fabricated PeLEDs with a peak luminance of 20420 cd m−2 and a fatigue half-life four times higher than original PeLEDs. This stable performance of the device can hasten the commercial development of PeLEDs.

(a) FTIR absorption spectrum (1550–1650 cm−1) of pristine CsxPbBry, pure P4VP, and P4VP–CsxPbBry composites; (b) Br–3d, Pb–4f orbital XPS spectra of pristine CsxPbBry, 3.5% P4VP–CsxPbBry and 7.0% P4VP–CsxPbBry; (c) FE-TEM of the corresponding products with diffraction patterns, with the crystalline planes represented in color; (d) XRD spectra of pristine CsxPbBry (2θ ranging from 10° to 50°) 3.5% P4VP–CsxPbBry, and 7.0% P4VP–CsxPbBry. The reference pattern associated with the observed peak of a multiphase pristine CsxPbBry and monophase P4VP–CsPbBr3.

Comparison of UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (under 9-V electric field excitation) of (a) pristine CsxPbBry and (b) 3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3. (c) Schematic of CsxPbBry surface charge damage caused by excess carrier density that is inhibited by generation of P4VP polaron region. (d) Magnified CIE spectra displaying EL stability of PeLEDs fabricated with pristine CsxPbBry and 3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3 as EML (centered at 1 V before and after highest experimentally observed luminance). (e) Energy utilization of PeLEDs fabricated with pristine CsxPbBry and 3.5% and 7.0% P4VP–CsPbBr3 as the EML.

(a) Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy spectra (Ecutoff and HOS) of different GQD contents (P-M: 0 mg, P-1: 1 mg, P-5: 5 mg, P-10: 10 mg) blended within the HTL–PEDOT:PSS. (b) Schematic of GQD blended within PEDOT:PSS to fine-tune the HOMO of HTL–PEDOT:PSS to align HOMO of EML. head.ce:title: Optimization of the carrier recombination and transmission properties in perovskite LEDs by doping poly (4-vinylpyridine) and graphene quantum dots made of chitin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We optimized the perovskite-emitting layer (EML) and hole transport layer (HTL) of perovskite light-emitting diodes (PeLEDs) by poly-4-vinylpyridine (P4VP) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to investigate the effects of carrier recombination and transport properties on the lifetime and performance of the devices. Finally, we successfully produced PeLEDs with a peak brightness of 20,420cd m−2, and the cycle lifetimes increased from 16 cycles to 100 cycles.

Light emission intensity and a lifetime of perovskite light-emitting diodes (PeLEDs) have improved considerably through fine-tuning the regulation of carriers’ recombination and transport. This study found that adding poly (4-vinylpyridine) within a CsxPbBry system leads to a dipole–dipole interaction of pyridine-N and Pb2+. This interaction increases the solubility of the CsxPbBry precursor solution, which stabilizes the CsxPbBry crystal structure and induces a CsPbBr3 unitary state. The interaction further generates a polaron region and causes the orderly distribution of excess carriers in the electric field, thus preventing disordered surface charges from structurally damaging the crystal. The PEDOT:PSS hole transport layer mixed with a graphene quantum dot made of chitin tunes the highest occupied molecular orbital to improve carrier transmission efficiency. We fabricated PeLEDs with a peak luminance of 20420 cd m−2 and a fatigue half-life four times higher than original PeLEDs. This stable performance of the device can hasten the commercial development of PeLEDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic–inorganic perovskite semiconductors have been developed rapidly with respect to adjustable energy levels [1], efficient carrier transport properties [2], and favorable optical properties [3]. These developments have led to the creation of solar cells [4-8], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [9-18], backlight modules [19-23], laser devices [24], flash photo memory [25,26], and resistive random-access memory [27]. Among these, perovskite LEDs (PeLEDs) have attracted the most attention, which has prompted nonlinear growth in the technological development of this field [28]. The perovskite-emitting layer, which has a full visible range of light (380–740 nm), can be prepared through the use of distinct dimensional crystal synthesis technologies (quantum dot [1,3,12,20,23], quasi-two-dimensional [11,28-31], and bulk [4-8]) and control of structural elements (ABX3 [32]).

To achieve high stability in PeLEDs, the emissive layer must be able to sustain high-intensity, long-term external energy excitation [11], which is dependent on the uniformity in the perovskite crystal size [33] and the crystal phase [24,34], and arrangement of the crystal [29]. In a three-dimensional (3D)-bulk inorganic perovskite system generated using the one-pot solution process, compared with low-dimensional perovskite, the 3D-bulk perovskite layer has a higher crystal dimension and exhibits more favorable performance under strong or long-term current excitation [8,13]. However, the disparate solubility of CsxPbBry and molar ratios of CsBr and PbBr2 lead to changes in their crystallization kinetics [10,19,24,34-38]; different single-crystal phases are mixed in the same crystal grain [10]. Because of the distinct phases of the single crystals (orthorhombic, cubic, and hexagonal) [39-41], the generation of such mixed crystals often results in defects forming in the stacked crystal grains [10,19]. When the carrier is excited, these defects allow excitons to easily fall into the excited-state trap and thereby cause nonradiative recombination, which destroys the crystal structure of the emissive layer and affects the low emission intensity and stability of electroluminescence (EL) [14,33]. Therefore, several studies have used the bonding forces of different compositions of organic ligands [30,31] or inorganic salts (ZnBr2 and NaBr) [11,42] as a means of overcoming the lower binding energies of the crystal phases and achieving uniform crystal phase growth in crystal grains.

Inorganic perovskites have poor film-forming abilities. Therefore, numerous studies have used a perovskite mixed with a polymer to compensate for the defects in the crystal grains and increase the film-forming properties of the emissive layer and thereby passivate the grain structure [14,19]. However, the improvement in film-forming properties achievable through conventional polymer blending methods can only solve the problem at the microlevel (crystal grain) of the emissive layer. To substantially improve the perovskite layer’s photoelectric properties and structure, changes must be made at the nanolevel of the crystal structure [7,8]. Therefore, Zuo et al. [7] proposed that the lone pair of electrons on the poly-4-vinylpyridine’s (P4VP) pyridine functional group be employed to produce a dipole interaction with Pb2+ in perovskite-MAPbI3 and integrated using a two-step solution spin-coating process to achieve an orderly arrangement of emissive layer crystal. This nanolevel process that improves the arrangement of crystal dramatically increased the energy conversion efficiency of perovskite solar cells. Other studies have suggested that high performance can be achieved in PeLEDs through use of an embedded polymer layer between the carrier transport and light-emitting layers as a buffer to block uncombined carriers from entering the next layer [15,16]; in this manner, electrons and holes are prevented from combining on the wrong surface (such as hole transport layer (HTL)–perovskite-emitting layer (EML) or ETL–EML) and reducing electrical damage.

Light-emitting diode layer engineering has also sought to regulate energy levels (valence band and conduction band) by horizontally fitting and aligning the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the emissive and carrier transport layers to the carrier’s transmission to efficiently achieve radiation recombination [13]. The main methods for achieving HOMO value alignment are the multilayer stacking method [6] and the orbital hybrid single-layer method [43]. However, the layer-by-layer method tends to cause excessive resistance at the junction of the stacked layers, which leads to poor charge transfer efficiency [44]. Therefore, the orbital hybrid single-layer method is ideal for HOMO value alignment projects. In this method, energy level is adjusted by dividing two materials of similar energy levels into a “host substance” (HS) and a “guest substance” (GS) and using the partial hybrid orbital to adjust the different ratios of the HS and GS and change the HOMO of the carrier transport layer [45-47]. A common HS for the HTL is poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS), which has favorable charge transport properties and hydrophilicity. The energy level of the HTL can easily be adjusted by doping a GS, such as organic small molecules or metal nanoparticles [6,45]. These methods for improvement have expanded the possibilities for use of the light emission capabilities of LEDs.

On the basis of the theory of the aforementioned studies, we propose that the interaction between a P4VP polymer and CsxPbBry can regulate crystal growth. Through this interaction, the P4VP polymer can perform both buffer and luminous functions in the same layer and protect the perovskite structure from the degradation caused by excessive current. To achieve alignment of the transport layer HOMO, we used graphene quantum dots (GQDs) made of chitin [48,49] as a GS to adjust the HOMO of the HS (PEDOT:PSS) and form a single transmission layer with an adjustable HOMO value to further improve the transmission efficiency of the carrier. The parameters were optimized to achieve high-performance, long-lifetime PeLEDs.

Cesium bromide (CsBr, >99.9%), lead bromide (PbBr2, 99%), poly(4-vinylpyridine) (P4VP, Mw ∼ 160,000), polystyrene (PS, Mw ∼ 192,000), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9%), methanol (MeOH, ≥99%), 2,2,2-(1,3,5-benzinetriyl)tris(1-phenyl-1H-benzimidazole) (TPBi, 99%), lithium Fluoride (LiF, ≥99.99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS, Al 4083, Ossila), aluminum (Al, Summit-Tech Co.). Patterned indium-tin-oxide (ITO) substrate (sheet resistance of 15 Ω) with a dimension of 30 * 30 * 0.7 mm3 was purchased from Lumtec. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), material and preparation is done in accordance of the reference [48,48]. All materials were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CsxPbBry perovskite and GQD-PEDOT:PSS precursor solutions preparation:

ce:section.ce:para: Fixed CsBr (0.24 mmol, ratio: 1.2), PbBr2 (0.2 mmol, ratio: 1), then add into 1 ml DMSO mix well 1 day.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Poly(4-vinylpyridine)(P4VP)-CsxPbBry precursor solutions by changing P4VP ratio substitution
ce:section.ce:para: Fixed CsBr (0.24 mmol, ratio: 1.2), PbBr2 (0.2 mmol, ratio: 1), then add separately P4VP (weight concentration 3.5%), P4VP (weight concentration 7%) into 1 ml DMSO mix well 1 day.
Fixed CsBr (0.24 mmol, ratio: 1.2), PbBr2 (0.2 mmol, ratio: 1) precursors are first added followed by addition of PS (weight concentration 3.5%) into 1 ml of DMSO under stirring for one day.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD-PEDOT:PSS precursor solutions preparation by changing GQDs ratio substitution
ce:section.ce:para: Addition of GQDs at different content 1 mg, 5 mg, 10 mg) with PEDOT:PSS mix by volume ratio 1:1.in 1 ml MeOH under stirring for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CsxPbBry perovskite and GQD-PEDOT:PSS thin film fabrication:
ce:section.ce:para: The glass substrate was sonicated with deionized water, acetone, and isopropanol for 15 min followed by oxygen plasma treatment for 5 min. Before spin-coating onto the washed glass substrate, the perovskite and GQD-PEDOT:PSS precursor solution was filtered with a 0.45 μm polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) filter. The GQD-PEDOT:PSS film spin-coating at 500 rpm for 5 s and 2000 rpm for 25 s, then annealed at 120 °C for 15 min. Afterward, the perovskite film was spin-coated at 3000 rpm for 45 s and annealed at 80 °C for 10 min. The perovskite thin film deposition processes were performed in a N2-filled glove box, whereas the HTL thin film processes were performed under ambient atmosphere. All processes were done at room temperature.

The hole-only devices structure is according to Kebin Lin et al’s research [50]. Patterned ITO indium-tin oxide coated glass substrates were sonicated with deionized water, acetone, and isopropanol for 15 min followed by oxygen plasma treatment for 5 min. First, the GQD-PEDOT:PSS of HTL precursor solution was filtered by a 0.45 μm PVDF filter and two-step spin-coated on ITO glass at 500 rpm for 5 s and 2000 rpm for 25 s, then annealing at 120 °C for 15 min. Second, the EML perovskite precursor solution was filtered by a 0.45 μm PVDF filter and spin-coated at 3000 rpm for 45 s, then annealing at 80 °C for 10 min. At last, Au (80 nm) were deposited by thermal evaporation under a high vacuum (∼10-6 Pa) environment.

Patterned ITO indium-tin oxide coated glass substrates were sonicated with deionized water, acetone, and isopropanol for 15 min followed by oxygen plasma treatment for 5 min. First, the GQD-PEDOT:PSS of HTL precursor solution was filtered by a 0.45 μm PVDF filter and two-step spin-coated on ITO glass at 500 rpm for 5 s and 2000 rpm for 25 s, then annealing at 120 °C for 15 min. Second, the EML perovskite precursor solution was filtered by a 0.45 μm PVDF filter and spin-coated at 3000 rpm for 45 s, then annealing at 80 °C for 10 min. At last, TPBi (30 nm), LiF (1 nm) and Al (100 nm) were deposited by thermal evaporation under a high vacuum (∼10-6 Pa) environment. The PeLEDs with an active area of 2 mm2 were encapsulated using epoxy glue and glass covers. The processes of HTL thin film deposition were performed under ambient atmosphere, whereas those of EML were completed in a N2-filled glove box.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of thin film and PeLEDs device
Perovskite and GQD-PEDOT:PSS thin film characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Interactions of P4VP-CsxPbBry and GQD-PEDOT:PSS were investigated using a FT-IR Spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Spectrum One). The binding energy of the P4VP-CsxPbBry were determined by XPS (Thermo VG-Scientific/Sigma probe). Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscopy (FE-TEM) image and EDS mapping were recorded using a JEOL JEM-2100F (at the Instrumentation Center, National Taiwan University), Field Emission Electron Microscope equipped with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) operated at 300 kV. The XRD analysis was characterized by PANalytical diffractometer. The optical excitation distribution of the perovskite was determined using PL excitation (PLE) spectroscopy (Nanolog, Horiba Scientific). The surface of perovskite thin film was coated with platinum and using Field Emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; Hitachi S4800) at an acceleration rate of 10 kV. TRPL spectra and Photoluminescence decay time were detected using a spectrometer (iHR320, HORIBA) coupled with a Hamamatsu C10910 streak camera; excitation source- PLP-10 Laser diode head-M10306-27 and an M10913 slow single-sweep unit at the National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC, Taiwan). UV–vis absorption spectra of the perovskite films were measured with a Jasco V-730 Spectrophotometer. The HOMO value of GQD-PEDOT:PSS was obtained from (CV; Autolab PGSTAT204). The UPS was obtained by Thermo VG-Scientific/Sigma Probe to double-verify the position of the HOMO value. Use (Phoenix300; SEO) to get the contact Angle of GQD-PEDOT:PSS. The morphologies of GQD-PEDOT:PSS using (AFM; Bruker Innova).

The electroluminescence spectra were collected using a SpectraScan PR670 photonic multichannel analyzer. The current density–voltage (IV) and luminance-voltage (LV) characteristics were taken using a Keithley source unit 2400 and a SpectraScan PR670 system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction between P4VP and CsxPbBry
ce:section.ce:para:
The underlying mechanism of the photoelectric properties of perovskite-emitting-layer (EML) optimization through controlled P4VP addition was investigated. The synergistic effect of P4VP was not only used as a polymeric bridge between perovskite grains to control their growth and development of passivate surface defects but also to control the photoelectric properties of the EML–CsxPbBry crystal. First, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; Fig. 1
a) revealed that the absorption wavenumber of pyridine-N red-shifted from 1559 cm−1 to 1603 cm−1 upon the addition of P4VP into the CsxPbBry solution, which vibration position indicates an electronic cloud had flowed between the pyridine-N and Pb2+. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) further supported this finding, as presented in Fig. 1 b and Fig. S1
a,b. Notably, the peak of the Pb metal signals was observed at a Pb 4f binding energy of CsxPbBry of approximately 137 eV and 141 eV ( Fig. 1 b) and disappeared upon addition P4VP. We hypothesize that the disappearance of the lead metal signal was due to the electronic cloud transferring from pyridine-N to Pb2+ through the dipole–dipole interaction. At the same time, the disappearance of the lead metal signal also indicates the bromide vacancies of CsPbxBry can be passivated by dipole–dipole interaction [51]. The binding energies of Br–3d, Pb–4f ( Fig. 1 b), and Cs–3d (
Fig. S1
b) of the CsxPbBry simultaneously shifted to higher binding energies, which indicates that the N-pyridine lone electron pair delocalized to the empty Pb2+ orbital like the electronic donor–acceptor system and formed a stable crystal structure. In previous research [51], part of the research considered the lone pair electrons in the polymer function group could passivate the CsPbxBry, which has under-coordinated Pb. Herein, we not only found that the “passivation of CsPbxBry” under the dipole–dipole interaction environment but also provides a significant change in the crystal phase composition of the grains and grain geometry ( Fig. 1 c). The effects of the interaction between the P4VP and CsxPbBry on the crystallization compositions are discussed in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The crystals composition homogeneity of EML
ce:section.ce:para:
The precursor CsxPbBry solution was prepared by mixing CsBr and PbBr2 at a molar ratio of 1.2:1, which is the routine for such a process. During the spin-coating annealing process, two main crystalline phases, namely CsPbBr3 and Cs4PbBr6 [36], underwent a rapid change in solubility because of the rapidity of perovskite crystallization, thereby forming a symbiotic CsxPbBry grain structure composed of CsPbBr3 (favorable solubility) and localized Cs4PbBr6 (poor solubility) [38]. This was confirmed through FE-TEM and XRD, as presented in Fig. 1 c,d, and Fig. S2 . The d-spacing of 0.698 nm and 0.423 nm of pristine perovskite corresponded to the diffraction signals of (10
2
¯
) hexagonal-Cs4PbBr6 [41] and (121) orthorhombic-CsPbBr3 [40]. The simultaneous presence of both peaks indicated that the CsPbBr3 and Cs4PbBr6 coexisted in the CsxPbBry structure ( Fig. 1 d). By contrast, the symbiotic CsxPbBry shifted to an orthorhombic-CsPbBr3 unitary state as the hexagonal-Cs4PbBr6 signals of (10
2
¯
) and (4
1
¯
2) disappeared, thereby revealing the P4VP-induced crystal orientation and modulated crystal growth. Furthermore, the solubility of the P4VP-based CsxPbBry solution increased substantially compared with the supersaturated pristine CsxPbBry solution (
Fig. S3
). We propose that the P4VP-based CsxPbBry solution acted as a “counterion” and improved the solubility of the precursor by promoting the dispersion of the cation (Pb2+) and assisting in inhibiting the Cs4PbBr6 crystal growth (
Fig. S4
) [52-54]. The underlying mechanism of the P4VP’s dispersal of ions involves the cation (Pb2+) in the precursor solution being distributed near the pyridine-N (strong Lewis base) because of the adsorption-like behavior of the dipole–dipole interaction; the medium-length P4VP (160000 MW) chain, which swells in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO; a good solvent for P4VP), was used to increase the steric stabilization and the dispersion of Pb2+ and Cs+ in the precursor solution, which thereby inhibited the generation of Cs4PbBr6 in the crystal phase (which has poor solubility) [55-57]. In addition, we observed that bromine site defects and lead site accumulation at the crystal grain surface in pristine CsxPbBry were suppressed after the addition of P4VP (
Fig. S5
a). The multiphase grain system containing a CsxPbBry symbiotic crystal phase tended to form a “trap hole” at the mismatching Cs4PbBr6 and CsPbBr3 interface, which further led to a decrease in electrical performance. Furthermore, according to the trap density calculation (nt) [33], the “trap holes” were effectively inhibited after P4VP-induced modulation of CsPbBr3 crystal phase (pristine CsPbxBry nt: 5.53*1017 cm−3; P4VP CsPbBr3 nt: 2.59*1017 cm−3) (
Fig. S5
b,c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photoelectric stability of pristine-CsxPbBry and P4VP-CsPbBr3
ce:section.ce:para:
The pristine CsxPbBry film exhibited high rates of grain growth and composition discontinuity upon scanning electron microscope observation (
Fig. S6
a). By contrast, the discontinuity of grain growth considerably improved and became more homogeneous after the addition of 3.5% P4VP into the CsxPbBry solution. Furthermore, the increase in grain density and smoothness was associated with a strong and uniform emission of photoluminescence. According to dynamic photoluminescence excitation (PLE) analysis ( Fig. 2
a), the pristine CsxPbBry exhibited multiexcitation sources ranging from 300 to 380 nm on the Y-axis-excitation wavelength. The pristine CsxPbBry demonstrated a discontinuity in the exciton sources of the hexagonal-Cs4PbBr6 and orthorhombic-CsPbBr3 surface mismatch stacking, which produced high electrical decoupling at the grain boundaries. The crystal homogeneity was simultaneously improved through P4VP-induced modulation, which suppressed trap holes and excessive grain boundaries in the thin-film system to get narrower PL-FWHM (pristine CsxPbBry: 19.92 nm to P4VP-CsPbBr3: 3.5%-15.02 nm and 7.0%-17.61 nm, Fig. 2 b). This was supported by the 3.5% P4VP-CsPbBr3 (Fig. 2 b, S6b and Table 1 ) exhibiting the longest photon lifetime-τavg in suppressing the nonradiative recombination of excitons. Further addition of P4VP negatively affected the perovskite. Although the 7.0% P4VP-CsPbBr3 exhibits the narrowest PLE range; however, this P4VP concentration parameter shows a lower PL-emission intensity and a shorter lifetime-τavg than 3.5% P4VP-CsPbBr3. An excess of P4VP causes the CsPbBr3 grains to segregate from P4VP and aggregate along the Z-axis of crystallization (
Fig. S6
a), which indicates that the nonradiative recombination of excitons increasingly originates from higher film roughness and heterogeneity. Therefore, an appropriate amount of P4VP can not only effectively control grain growth but also reduce and passivate grain boundaries for enhanced monosourced radiative recombination of the CsPbBr3.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption was investigated under 9-V electric field excitation (
Fig. S7
a) to enable observation of the electrical stability of CsxPbBry and P4VP–CsPbBr3; this was followed by differential processing of the peak band of the 3D-bulk absorption characteristics to monitor changes in the absorption spectrum. As illustrated in Fig. 3
a, the pristine CsxPbBry was associated with uncontrolled charge carrier recombination as the inflection point of the peak of 3D-bulk absorption shifted to a high wavelength upon long excitation (first inflection, from without 9-V excitation to 9-V excitation 10 min: 510 to 512 nm; second inflection, from without 9-V excitation to 9-V excitation 10 min: 522 to 523 nm). The derivation absorption spectroscopy slope also demonstrated a notable change (Table S1). The excess of carriers penetrated through the surface structure to further damage the multiphase perovskite crystal stability and cause the absorption state to become red-shifted, which indicated crystal degradation. Notably, the inflection point displacement was mitigated by an addition of 3.5% P4VP; this indicates the P4VP suppressed charge leakage within the crystal structure and preserved the structural stability of the perovskite ( Fig. 3 b). We propose that the P4VP–CsPbBr3 resistance to surface charge damage originates from the interaction between pyridine-N and Pb2+ forming a polaron region under an applied electric field (where the pyridine-N is the partial negative charge and Pb2+ the partial positive charge) [58]. The formation of a polaron region induces a neat and uniform charge distribution that improves electrical stability ( Fig. 3 c). Moreover, we noted red-shifting of the spectrum signal inflection point in the polystyrene (PS)–CsxPbBry system due to the absence of polaron formation at the surface area of the perovskite (
Fig. S7
b), which supports the notion that polymeric moieties free of a functional group are associated with the absence of a dipole–dipole interaction. Therefore, the presence of polarons is necessary to suppress charge carrier diffusion within the crystal lattice damaging the perovskite lattice. Table S2 and Fig. 3 d indicate that PeLEDs constructed with 3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3 as the EML not only exhibit maximum luminance (3006 cd m−2) but demonstrate improved electroluminescence (EL) stability compared with those constructed with pristine CsxPbBry. Although PeLEDs constructed with 7.0% P4VP–CsPbBr3 also possess polaron regions, the rough grains stacked in the Z-axis direction cause the luminance to be weaker than that produced through PeLEDs with 3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3. Notably, the EL of 7.0% P4VP–CsPbBr3 also remains stable because of the presence of a polaron region (
Fig. S7
c). The polaron region formed by P4VP and CsPbBr3 not only inhibits crystal damage induced by the unrecombined surface charge but also improves the optoelectronic energy utilization of PeLEDs. As presented in Fig. 3 e, the energy use of PeLEDs with a polaron region is 10 times higher than that of PeLEDs without a polaron region. This is likely because the polaron region controls the flow of excess carriers, which improves the rate of radiation recombination. Herein, we optimized the P4VP concentration in P4VP–CsPbBr3 to increase the optoelectronic stability of PeLEDs by controlling the formation of polaron regions. Nevertheless, we believe the luminance of PeLEDs can be further optimized through bandgap alignment engineering. We sought to adjust the HOMO of the HTL alignment with the EML. Hence, we pursued HOMO calibration of the HTL to tune the energy barrier at the HTL and EML interface through the addition of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The HOMO value alignment of HTL-ETL
ce:section.ce:para:
We sought to use bandgap alignment engineering by doping semiconductor materials with a deep HOMO value into the HTL HOMO of the host PEDOT:PSS. We blended GQDs into PEDOT:PSS to match the bandgap with 3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3 EML HOMO in order to optimize the carrier’s transmission and achieve high performance of green light emission. A GQD of a carboxylic acid functional group was synthesized from chitin, as described previously (46). According to Du et al. [59], the π–π interaction and hydrophilic interaction originate from the carboxylic acid-GQD doping PEDOT:PSS system. Here, the phenomena of π–π interaction and hydrophilic interaction were imaged and confirmed using FTIR signal shifts. The C = C signals of the carbon ring (GQDs) and thiophene (PEDOT) were separately located at 1586 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1, respectively (
Fig. S8
a). Upon formation of the PEDOT:PSS–GQD blend, both C = C signals shifted to the unique peak of 1581 cm−1 that supported the presence of a highly hybrid π–π orbital. Hydrophilic interactions occurred because of the formation of hydrogen bonds (H-bonds) between the − SO3H (PSS) and COOH ligand (GQDs). The H-bond signal shifted from 1211 cm−1 to 1206 cm−1 (
Fig. S8
b), which indicates a hydrophilic interaction occurred between the − SO3H and carboxylic acid. Cyclic voltammetry (C–V) (
Fig. S9
) revealed that the PEDOT:PSS HOMO position was influenced by GQD loading. The increase in the HOMO level correlated with an increase in the GQD doping from P-M, P-1, P-5, and P-10. Additionally, a highest occupied state (HOS) and Ecutoff shifting was observed ( Fig. 4
a). As an explanation of the aforementioned phenomenon, we propose that efficient HOMO regulation originated from the valence band orbital hybridization caused by the π–π interaction of C = C and that the hydrophilic interaction allowed GQDs to be uniformly dispersed in PEDOT:PSS. We used the aforementioned mechanism to fine-tune the HOMO position by increasing the GQD (HOMO: −5.91 eV) loading to set the PEDOT:PSS HOMO position from P-M: −5.08 eV to P-5: −5.48 eV. We achieved excellent carrier transport through the horizontal alignment of the HTL and EML HOMO values (HTL: −5.48 eV, EML: −5.54 eV; Fig. 4 b). In the layer-by-layer process of LEDs, the adhesiveness of the HTL to the EML is key in controlling and suppressing the trap site at the interface. We compared the water contact angles of HTL candidates with similar HOMO values (
Fig. S10
), which were as follows: poly-TPD: 81.80°; PEDOT:PSS–GQD: 11.50°–14.86°. We found that the super hydrophilicity of our system originated from the presence of − OH and − COOH functional groups of GQDs, causing the substrate/Air's surface energy promotion. The high surface energy was associated with improved adhesion between layers and further reduced the generation of interface resistance, thereby creating improved smoothness and generating smaller grains [60] to reduce trap site localization in the deposited P4VP–CsPbBr3 EML on the HTL of PEDOT:PSS–GQD. However, an excess addition of GQDs not only causes GQD aggregation, which increases the surface roughness of HTL ( Fig. 4 c), but also increases HTL conductivity (
Fig. S11
a), which may cause an imbalanced injection of charge carriers into EML. Simultaneously, GQD aggregation also causes GQD to generate a conductor phenomenon so that the HOMO value of P-10 cannot obtain deeper values due to the addition of GQD. According to hole-only devices measurement (
Fig. S11
b), [50] it can further know the carrier transport characteristics. At the low voltage (0–3 V) measurement environment, P-1 shows unstable carrier transport characteristics; P-5 has relatively stable carrier transport; P-10 is consistent with the previous problem with high conductivity. Meanwhile, the carrier transport characteristics of P-10 at high voltage show a high resistance phenomenon caused by the disintegration of the EML layer. By tuning GQD loading (P-M, P-1, P-5, and P-10) in the HTL, we constructed a PeLED device with 3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3 as the EML. We found that optimizing GQD doping in PEDOT:PSS led to the luminance increasing from 3006 cd m−2 (P-M) to 20420 cd m−2 (P-5) ( Fig. 4 d). As predicted, in the device constructed with P-10, an excessive current density before turn-on voltage was associated with a decrease in PeLED performance (
Fig. S11
c,d). Additionally, the effect of the polaron region inhibiting excess carriers can be supported by the absence of significant differences in P-1 to P-10′s current density after operating voltage (4.5 V) (
Fig. S11
c). By adjusting the HTL HOMO of PEDOT:PSS through blending GQDs to match the EML HOMO of 3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3 and regulating the carrier’s transmission distance with an appropriate thickness (
Fig. S12
a,b) to reach the device performance optimization ( Table 2
).

The EML (CsPbBr3) and HTL (PEDOT:PSS) optimized with P4VP and GQDs were integrated into PeLED devices for measurement of their on–off cycle lifetime ( Fig. 5
a). We fabricated PeLED devices of the following structures: indium-tin-oxide (ITO)/P–M PEDOT–PSS/CsxPbBry (black)/2,2′,2′'-(1,3,5-benzinetriyl)-tris(1-phenyl-1-H-benzimidazole) (TPBi)/LiF-Al, ITO/P–M PEDOT–PSS/3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3 (blue)/TPBi/LiF-Al, and ITO/P–5 PEDOT–PSS GQDs/3.5% PVP–CsPbBr3 (red)/TPBi/LiF-Al. We observed that the on–off cycle lifetimes increased from 16 cycles in pristine CsxPbBry to 100 cycles with an intensity of 594 cd m−2 for P-5/3.5% P4VP–CsPbBr3. At the same time, the P-5/3.5% P4VP-CsPbBr3 device was observed to have a three-fold higher half-life than P-M/pristine CsxPbBry under long-term voltage (3.4 V) excitation (P-M/pristine CsxPbBry: 15 min to P-5/3.5% P4VP-CsPbBr3: 45 min) (
Fig. S13
a), then the half-period of 100 cd m−2 (T50) also a nearly twenty-fold increase (
Fig. S13
b). This finding was attributed to the dual effect of P4VP and GQDs in the perovskite charge carrier mechanism and the bandgap alignment. The first underlying reason for a sustainable on–off working lifetime of over 100 cycles was the improvement in charge carriers recombination, which occurred because of the synergistic effect of P4VP blended with CsxPbBry growing into a homocrystalline CsPbBr3 pure phase and the formation of a polaron region at the P4VP–perovskite interface. The second reason was the effect of efficient and balanced charge carrier transport and injection properties on the PeLED luminance performance. The charge carrier injection was optimized through the GQDs adjusting the HOMO of the HTL–PEDOT:PSS to match that of the EML. In Fig. 5 b, a Commission International de l’Eclairage (CIE) chromatic plot displays the variation in light coordinates following the on–off lifetime cycle test for each of the devices. We found the spectral stability of the PeLEDs to correlate with the device’s ability to sustain a high number of on–off cycles. This result illustrates that the red-shifting of the EL (which represents the degradation of the crystal structure due to the change in radiative recombination pathways from excitons and thermal activated centers) [61,62] was suppressed after the EML and HTL were optimized. Therefore, the PeLEDs prepared after optimization exhibited outstanding performance, long working lifetime, and high spectral stability.

We optimized the HTL and EML of PeLEDs to examine the effects of carrier recombination and transport properties on the lifetime and performance of the devices. From the result of mixing P4VP and CsPbBr3 in the EML, the dipole–dipole interaction produced by pyridine-N and Pb2+ were observed 1) to increase the solubility of the precursor solution, which allowed the crystallization mechanism to achieve a highly stable and unitary orthorhombic-CsPbBr3 state, and 2) to form a polaron region under an electric field, which polarized uncomplexed carriers, thereby protecting the crystal structure from surface charge damage and allowing it to conduct effective radiation recombination. To modify the HTL and allow for HOMO alignment, we adopted GQDs with a lower HOMO to reduce the HOMO of the HTL (with the main body of PEDOT:PSS) and achieve high-efficiency hole transmission at double-layer HOMO alignment. On the basis of the effects of the aforementioned modifications, we can conclude that radiation recombination of carriers most strongly affects the stability and lifetime of PeLEDs. Furthermore, the transport properties of carriers play an essential role in improving luminance. Using the optimized parameters, we successfully produced PeLEDs with a peak brightness of 20420 cd m−2 that are able to sustain brightness up to 594 cd m−2 after 100 uses. The long-life, high-performance PeLEDs of this study can be used to improve commercial PeLEDs products.
